version: AI 100-1 v1.0 (Jan 2023) + AI 600-1 GenAI Profile (Jul 2024) — curated excerpts

entries.text: Legal and regulatory requirements involving AI are understood, managed, and documented.
entries.guidance: Identify and inventory all federal, state, sector-specific, and international laws or regulations (e.g., EU AI Act, sectoral rules) that apply to the AI system. Track changes over time and assign ownership of compliance for each obligation.

entries.text: The characteristics of trustworthy AI are integrated into organizational policies, processes, procedures, and practices.
entries.guidance: Operationalize the trustworthy-AI characteristics: valid and reliable, safe, secure and resilient, accountable and transparent, explainable and interpretable, privacy-enhanced, and fair (with managed bias).

entries.text: Roles, responsibilities, and lines of communication related to mapping, measuring, and managing AI risks are documented.
entries.guidance: Assign a clear accountable owner for the AI system. Document who can pause, modify, or shut down the system, and the escalation path for incidents.

entries.text: Organizational practices are in place to foster a critical thinking and safety-first mindset in the design, development, deployment, and uses of AI systems.
entries.guidance: Establish risk tolerance thresholds before deployment. For high-impact federal use cases (e.g., security, law enforcement), default to lower tolerance and require independent review.

entries.text: Intended purposes, potentially beneficial uses, context-specific laws, norms, expectations, and prospective settings in which the AI system will be deployed are understood and documented.
entries.guidance: Document the deployment context in detail: who the users are, what decisions the system informs or automates, the affected populations, and the alternatives that already exist.

entries.text: The specific tasks and methods used to implement the tasks that the AI system will support are defined.
entries.guidance: Specify the system's tasks at a granularity that lets reviewers evaluate them independently (e.g., 'classify alert severity' vs. 'auto-close low-confidence alerts').

entries.text: Potential benefits of intended AI system functionality and performance are examined and documented.
entries.guidance: State the benefits in measurable terms (analyst throughput, mean time to triage, missed-alert rate) and compare to the non-AI baseline.

entries.text: Approaches for mapping AI risks to use, misuse, and abuse are established.
entries.guidance: Enumerate failure modes (false negatives missing real intrusions, false positives flooding analysts), misuse (analysts deferring uncritically to AI output), and abuse (adversaries crafting inputs to evade detection).

entries.text: AI system is evaluated regularly for safety risks.
entries.guidance: Run adversarial-input testing, red-team exercises, and tabletop scenarios at a frequency appropriate to the risk tier. For high-risk deployments, this should be at least quarterly and after any model update.

entries.text: AI system security and resilience are evaluated and documented.
entries.guidance: Test for prompt injection, jailbreaks, training-data extraction, and model denial-of-service. Document detection coverage and known gaps.

entries.text: Fairness and bias — as identified in the MAP function — are evaluated and results are documented.
entries.guidance: Measure performance across affected subgroups (e.g., alert classification accuracy by source IP region or asset criticality tier). Investigate disparate error rates.

entries.text: Responses to the AI risks deemed high priority, as identified by the MAP function, are developed, planned, and documented.
entries.guidance: For each top-priority risk, document the mitigation, the residual risk after mitigation, and the trigger that would force a re-evaluation.

entries.text: Post-deployment AI system monitoring plans are implemented, including mechanisms for capturing and evaluating input from users and other relevant AI actors, appeal and override, decommissioning, incident response, and change management.
entries.guidance: Implement continuous monitoring (drift, performance, anomaly), an appeals channel for affected parties, a documented override path, and an incident-response runbook with thresholds for partial or full shutdown.

entries.text: Processes are in place to determine the needed level of risk management activities based on the organization's risk tolerance, including for generative AI.
entries.guidance: Generative-AI systems (LLM-based agents, RAG, summarizers) require additional controls for confabulation, data leakage, and IP/PII exposure. Calibrate effort to the actual decision authority the model has.
